narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, an LSAT score of 27, upper-middle family income, and your plan to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 63.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile that stood out as the strongest influences. Most critically, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—well below the typical student's rank around the middle of their class—pushed the prediction heavily toward expecting bar failure, as it signals weaker performance relative to peers at a pivotal stage. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also contributed in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles compared to the average around decile 6. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA stood out as the one factor pulling strongly in favor of bar passage success, counteracting some of the risks but not enough to shift the overall outlook. Compared to typical students who pass the bar on their first try, your decile rankings are notably lower, while your GPA is below the usual 3.21 average, and even your LSAT trails the common 37-point benchmark—though full-time status aligns with the vast majority who succeed. These top influences outweighed other details like your family background, leading the model to see higher risk in your path to bar success. It's tough to hear, especially when you're motivated to build a legal career, but this isn't a judgment on your potential—it's a data-driven flag based on historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your upper-level grades if possible through additional coursework, or exploring programs with bridge support for students from similar backgrounds. You might also seek feedback from admissions counselors on targeted prep for the bar or apply to schools with holistic reviews that weigh personal stories like yours more heavily. With focused improvements, you can reshape this prediction and open new doors.
